item.title: Prince George’s County’s Democratic Primary Voters See Room for Improvement On Housing-Related Issues
item.dc:creator: Abby Springs
item.description: Democratic primary voters in Prince George’s County see room for improvement when it comes to the County Council’s handling of housing costs, age and quality of housing, and road safety.
item.content:encoded:
In Prince George’s County, Maryland, housing costs have increased as the population has grown, leaving residents to contend with financial strain, housing shortages, and an aging housing stock.

New polling from Data for Progress and Greater Greater Washington shows that Democratic primary voters in Prince George’s County see room for improvement when it comes to the County Council’s handling of housing costs, age and quality of housing, and road safety, but few voters contact their members about issues in the county, especially when it comes to housing or zoning. Nearly all voters would like the Council and executives to redesign streets to improve road safety for all users, even if it slows drivers down, and 3 in 4 say the age and quality of housing is an issue. Voters want to see improvements to the streets and housing, even if they are unlikely to contact the Council. 

Democratic primary voters clearly want the Council to redesign streets with a focus on improving road safety rather than speed. This is a priority for nearly all voters, regardless of age or homeownership. 

   

Most respondents disapprove of how the County Council has handled the cost of living, especially those under the age of 45 (66%) and renters (59%). Half of homeowners also disapprove of the Council’s handling of the cost of living in Prince George’s County.

   

But despite that disapproval, only about a quarter of Democratic primary voters in Prince George’s County have written to their County Council member about local issues, while the vast majority (73%) have not. 

   

Additionally, among those who have written to the County Council, the majority have not written about housing or zoning issues.

   

Despite low engagement with the County Council, Democratic primary voters still have clear priorities when it comes to lowering housing costs. Most respondents (57%) say it is more important to lower housing costs than to preserve neighborhood character. Younger voters (73%) and renters (64%) are even more likely to prioritize lower housing costs, and a majority of homeowners (52%) say the same.

   

Most Democratic primary voters see the age and quality of housing in the county as either a minor (42%) or major (32%) issue, with only one-fifth saying it is not really an issue at all. 

   

However, concerns about the cost, age, and quality of housing in Prince George’s County do not necessarily translate into support for new development. Democratic primary voters are split on whether a County Council candidate’s support for a new housing development in their neighborhood would make them more or less likely to vote for that member’s reelection.

By a +2-point margin, these voters say they would be more likely to vote for a councilor who supports new housing in their neighborhood, while 23% say it would make no difference in their vote. A plurality of younger voters (40%) and renters (41%) are more likely to vote for such a candidate, while homeowners say they are less likely to do so, by a -3-point net margin.

   

Democratic primary voters are more comfortable with new housing the farther away it’s built, with at least 3 in 5 voters somewhat or very comfortable having new housing built in their ZIP code (60%), elsewhere in the county (73%), or outside the county (69%). These voters are less comfortable (a little or not comfortable at all) about new housing on their block (56%), and they are split on housing in their neighborhood or subdivision. 

This polling shows that Democratic primary voters in Prince George’s County, especially older voters and homeowners, still harbor a degree of not-in-my-backyard (NIMBY) attitudes that limit support for new housing in certain areas, creating a barrier when it comes to providing more affordable and higher-quality housing. However, most of these voters overall want the County Council to do more to lower the cost of living, and care more about affordable housing than neighborhood character.

Survey Methodology

From March 21 to 30, 2026, Data for Progress conducted a survey of 389 Democratic primary voters in Prince George's County, Maryland using SMS and web panel respondents. The sample was weighted to be representative of Democratic primary voters by age, gender, education, race, geography, and recalled presidential vote. The survey was conducted in English. The margin of error associated with the sample size is ±5 percentage points. Results for subgroups of the sample are subject to increased margins of error. For more information please visit dataforprogress.org/our-methodology

media:content.media:title: Prince George’s County’s Democratic Primary Voters See Room for Improvement On Housing-Related Issues

item.title: Voters Say Their Top Priority in the Iran War Is Ending It
item.dc:creator: Lew Blank
item.description: A majority of voters (52%) — including 60% of Independents — think the U.S. is not accomplishing its goals in Iran.
item.content:encoded:
On April 17, a day after a 10-day ceasefire was reached between Israel and Lebanon, President Donald Trump declared that the Strait of Hormuz would be “completely open and ready for business.” Oil prices dropped by nearly 12%, and Trump stated that the war in Iran “should be ending pretty soon.”

While this seemed like a turning point, just one day later Iran reinstated its closure of the Strait of Hormuz after the Trump administration maintained its blockade on Iranian ports. Days later, Iran seized two cargo ships in the strait. Oil prices in the U.S. are now at their highest level since the beginning of the Iran war.

In a new survey, Data for Progress finds that a majority of voters (52%) — including 60% of Independents — think the U.S. is not accomplishing its goals in Iran. Only 35% of voters think the U.S. is accomplishing its goals in the war.

   

Next, voters were shown five potential goals for the U.S. regarding the Iran war, and were asked which should be the main goal. 

A plurality of voters (39%) — including 56% of Democrats and 42% of Independents — say the main goal should be to “end the war as soon as possible.” 

About 1 in 4 voters (23%) — including a plurality of Republicans (36%) — think the top goal should be to prevent Iran from possessing a nuclear weapon. Smaller percentages prioritize reopening the Strait of Hormuz (13%), dismantling Iran’s regional military network (11%), and changing the Iranian regime to a more U.S.-aligned government (7%).

   

Together, these findings indicate that a majority of voters think the Trump administration is not succeeding in the Iran war, and their top priority is to end the war as soon as possible.

Survey Methodology

From April 24 to 27, 2026, Data for Progress conducted a survey of 1,167 U.S. likely voters nationally using web panel respondents. The sample was weighted to be representative of likely voters by age, gender, education, race, geography, and recalled presidential vote. The survey was conducted in English. The margin of error associated with the sample size is ±3 percentage points. Results for subgroups of the sample are subject to increased margins of error. Partisanship reflected in tabulations is based on self-identified party affiliation, not partisan registration. For more information please visit dataforprogress.org/our-methodology.

media:content.media:title: Voters Say Their Top Priority in the Iran War Is Ending It

item.title: Voters Support Multiple Pro-Worker Measures to Regulate AI
item.dc:creator: Lew Blank
item.description: Overwhelming majorities of voters agree that “workers should know if AI is being used to monitor their work” (+76-point net margin).
item.content:encoded:
Artificial intelligence (AI) could have a massive impact on the U.S. economy in the coming years, with some analysts predicting catastrophic job loss as AI replaces human workers, and others raising concerns about companies using AI to closely monitor the minutiae of workers’ activities on the job.

In a new survey, Data for Progress examined which institutions voters trust to protect workers from AI in the workplace, and tested public opinion on multiple measures that would improve workers’ rights with regard to AI.

Voters were first asked about their general levels of trust for five different groups. The only tested group that a majority of voters trust is labor unions (59%). Less than half of voters trust Democrats (48%), Republicans (45%), corporations (36%), and politicians (27%).

   

Voters were then asked which of four groups — labor unions, Democrats, Republicans, or employers — they think would be most likely to protect workers from the potential harms of AI in the workplace.

A plurality of voters (27%) — including a plurality of Independents (36%) — trust labor unions most to protect workers from AI, followed by Democrats (23%), Republicans (19%), and employers (12%). Pluralities of Democratic and Republican voters place the most trust in their respective parties.

   

Finally, voters were asked whether they agree or disagree with multiple measures to increase worker control over AI in the workplace and require employers to be transparent about how they use AI.

Overwhelming majorities of voters agree that “workers should know if AI is being used to monitor their work” (+76-point net margin), that “AI should not be used by employers unless there is meaningful human oversight” (+68), and that “if an employer uses AI to become more profitable, its workers should receive better pay or benefits” (+67).

A strong majority of voters also agree that “workers should be involved in deciding how their employer uses AI” (+54). 

In a split test, voters were asked whether they agree or disagree that employers using AI to monitor workers in all aspects of their jobs is a violation of “their privacy” (Split A) or “their freedom” (Split B). Voters agree that this is a violation of both — +55 and +45, respectively.

   

Together, these results indicate that voters trust labor unions most to protect workers from the potential harms of AI in the workplace, and strongly back multiple measures to increase workers’ decision-making power and employers’ transparency regarding the use of AI.

Survey Methodology

From April 17 to 20, 2026, Data for Progress conducted a survey of 1,085 U.S. likely voters nationally using web panel respondents. The sample was weighted to be representative of likely voters by age, gender, education, race, geography, and recalled presidential vote. The survey was conducted in English. The margin of error associated with the sample size is ±3 percentage points. Results for subgroups of the sample are subject to increased margins of error. Partisanship reflected in tabulations is based on self-identified party affiliation, not partisan registration. For more information please visit dataforprogress.org/our-methodology.

media:content.media:title: Voters Support Multiple Pro-Worker Measures to Regulate AI

item.title: Voters Support a Progressive Agenda Focused on Lowering Costs and Taking on Corporations
item.dc:creator: Lew Blank
item.description: Requiring two weeks of paid time off for all full-time workers is supported by 79% of voters.
item.content:encoded:
Donald Trump won the 2024 election on a pledge to lower costs, but the cost of living has gone up for many American families under his presidency.  From skyrocketing gas prices to rising energy costs to increasing healthcare premiums, the electorate is understandably sour on the state of the economy. 

In a new survey, Data for Progress tested 11 policies being championed by the Congressional Progressive Caucus, aimed at lowering costs and reducing the power of large corporations.  The slate of policies spans worker protections, consumer costs, corporate accountability, housing affordability, and campaign finance reform.

Every policy tested earns majority support from at least 3 in 5 voters. Requiring two weeks of paid time off for all full-time workers and restricting private utility companies from passing unreasonable costs on to customers are the most popular policies on the list — each earning support from 79% of voters.

Voters also show strong support for reducing corporate control over seed patents (75% support), capping super PAC donations (71%), banning artificial intelligence from setting prices or wages based on personal data (70%), and enabling federal generic drug manufacturing (70%).

Policies that earn support from at least 65% of voters also include taxing unusually high oil company profits to provide a gas rebate (69% support), raising overtime pay (69%), investing $1 trillion in affordable housing (66%), cracking down on grocery store price gouging (65%), and capping childcare costs (65%). 

See the survey crosstabs for the full wording tested for each policy.

   

Support for these policies crosses party lines, with each receiving majority support among Democrats, Independents, and Republicans. 

   

The survey also finds strong support for these policies among key voting blocs, including persuadable voters (voters who say they vote about equally for both major parties), Independent voters, voters who live in a swing district, and those who voted for Trump in 2024.

   

Broadly, these findings indicate that a progressive agenda focused on lowering costs and reining in the power of large corporations commands majority support with voters across the political spectrum, including Republicans and the persuadable voters who could play a key role in the upcoming midterm elections.

Survey Methodology

From April 17 to 20, 2026, Data for Progress conducted a survey of 1,085 U.S. likely voters nationally using web panel respondents. The sample was weighted to be representative of likely voters by age, gender, education, race, geography, and recalled presidential vote. The survey was conducted in English. The margin of error associated with the sample size is ±3 percentage points. Results for subgroups of the sample are subject to increased margins of error. Partisanship reflected in tabulations is based on self-identified party affiliation, not partisan registration. For more information please visit dataforprogress.org/our-methodology.

"Swing district voters" are those who live in a DCCC frontline district or DCCC district in play.

"Persuadable voters" are those who say they vote about equally for both parties.

media:content.media:title: Voters Support a Progressive Agenda Focused on Lowering Costs and Taking on Corporations

item.title: Voters Prefer Cooperating Over Competing With China to Enable Faster Progress on Clean Energy Technologies
item.dc:creator: Abby Springs
item.description: While voters may view China as more of an adversary, they believe we’ll make progress on clean energy technologies faster by prioritizing cooperation over competition.
item.content:encoded:
As China rapidly outpaces the rest of the world in deploying clean energy and climate technologies, new polling from Data for Progress examines U.S. likely voter attitudes toward China in the context of climate change, clean energy technologies, and the energy transition. 

By a 37-point margin, U.S. voters have an unfavorable view of China, with only 25% expressing a favorable opinion and 62% expressing an unfavorable one. This level of favorability is comparable with perceptions of China among U.S. adults writ large, which Pew Research Center finds have become more positive in recent years. In contrast, at least 40% of U.S. voters say they have a favorable view of India (41%) and Mexico (45%). 

By an even larger 58-point margin, Americans think the United States’ relationship with China is more adversarial than collegial, with 74% of voters believing “China is more of an adversary and competitor of the U.S.” and just 16% of voters believing “China is more of an ally and partner.” Notably, however, voters under 45 and Democrats consider China an ally and partner at higher rates than their older, Independent, and Republican counterparts.

   

The survey next asked voters whether they believe the clean energy transition would proceed faster if the U.S. prioritizes cooperating or competing with China. A plurality of voters (47%), including majorities of Democrats (58%), Independents (52%), and voters under 45 (55%), agree more with a statement saying, “We’ll make progress on clean energy technologies faster if the United States prioritizes cooperating with China on the development and manufacturing of these technologies,” while 39% say we’ll make faster progress if we prioritize competition.

   

China is considered the world leader in the development and maturation of clean energy technologies, including electric vehicles (EVs) and solar. However, when U.S. voters are asked whether they think the U.S. or China is the global leader in developing clean energy technologies, a slim majority of Americans (52%) choose the U.S over China (31%). This split is largest among Republicans, with 69% of Republicans claiming that the U.S. leads the world.

   

With Chinese EVs soon to be available in Canada, we next set out to understand Americans’ openness to purchasing an EV and specifically a Chinese EV, should they become available in the U.S. 

Overall, voters say they’re not likely to buy an EV — regardless of where it’s made — within the next decade. This includes 48% of voters who say they’re unlikely to buy an EV made in the U.S. (N = 594), 56% who say they’re unlikely to buy an EV made elsewhere, and 60% who say they’re unlikely to buy a Chinese EV made in the U.S. (N = 619).

   

Once informed of the average price of an EV in the U.S. today ($56,000) and an expected price of Chinese EVs in Canada ($36,000), 41% of voters say they’re likely to buy an EV built in the U.S. (N=594), and around 1 in 3 voters (34%) say they’re likely to buy a Chinese EV made in the U.S. (N=619) or an EV made elsewhere. 

When asked if Americans should be able to purchase Chinese EVs in the U.S., voters are split, with a plurality (46%) saying they oppose making Chinese EVs available and 41% saying they support doing so. Once again, Democrats and voters under 45 are more supportive of making Chinese EVs available domestically.

   

These findings demonstrate that U.S. voters are largely unaware of China’s dominance in clean energy and electric vehicle technologies. While voters may view China as more of an adversary and competitor overall, they believe we’ll make progress on clean energy technologies faster by prioritizing cooperation with China over competition. 

Additionally, while voters say they are not inclined to purchase an EV — whether made by a Chinese firm or not — they’re split on whether Chinese EVs should be made available to American consumers in general. Democrats and voters under 45 demonstrate greater awareness of China’s leadership on clean energy and openness to making Chinese EVs available in the U.S., suggesting a correlation between the two. 

Survey Methodology

From April 3 to 6, 2026, Data for Progress conducted a survey of 1,213 U.S. likely voters nationally using web panel respondents. The sample was weighted to be representative of likely voters by age, gender, education, race, geography, and recalled presidential vote. The survey was conducted in English. The margin of error associated with the sample size is ± 3 percentage points. Results for subgroups of the sample are subject to increased margins of error. Partisanship reflected in tabulations is based on self-identified party affiliation, not partisan registration. For more information please visit dataforprogress.org/our-methodology.

media:content.media:title: Voters Prefer Cooperating Over Competing With China to Enable Faster Progress on Clean Energy Technologies

item.title: This Earth Day, Two-Thirds of Voters Say Ensuring Clean Air and Water for All Should Be a Top Priority
item.dc:creator: Lew Blank
item.description: This survey finds that a majority of voters (54%) disapprove of the way Trump is handling climate change and the environment, including 85% of Democrats and 58% of Independents.
item.content:encoded:
On April 22, 1970, roughly 20 million Americans — 10% of the U.S. population at the time — took to the streets to protest against pollution, marking the first Earth Day.

New polling from Data for Progress finds that more than 60% of U.S. voters say Earth Day is important to them personally, including majorities of Democrats (73%), Independents (60%), and Republicans (53%).

Support for protecting the Earth’s air and water is also high across party lines. A strong, bipartisan majority of voters (66%) think ensuring every community has access to clean air and water should be a top priority for the U.S.

   

Despite broad public support for protecting clean air and water, the Trump administration has touted its rollbacks of environmental rules as the “biggest deregulatory action in U.S. history,” with the administration proposing cuts to rules that limit mercury and coal ash from power plants and eliminating caps on vehicle emissions. A majority of voters (51%), including 60% of Independents, do not trust the Trump administration to ensure clean air and water for all Americans.

   

During his second term, Trump has sought to reverse much of the climate action taken by the Biden administration — including by slashing funding and programs that incentivized clean energy, ending the government’s ability to regulate greenhouse gas emissions in certain sectors, and pushing fossil fuel production over renewable energy. 

This survey finds that a majority of voters (54%) disapprove of the way Trump is handling climate change and the environment, including 85% of Democrats and 58% of Independents.

   

When voters are asked whether they think the U.S. is on the right or wrong track when it comes to addressing climate change, a majority (53%), including strong majorities of Democrats (75%) and Independents (63%), say the U.S. is on the wrong track. This is consistent with voter attitudes reported a year ago. Only Republicans believe that the U.S. is on the right track (64%).

   

Respondents were next asked to choose between two statements related to the U.S.’s role in global climate leadership, with a bipartisan majority (68%) selecting the following statement: “The United States should take ambitious action to address climate change, even if other countries do not. We should lead the world on this issue and set the example for other countries to follow suit.” 

In contrast, just 25% of voters prefer the following statement: “If other countries do not take action to address climate change, the United States should not either. It is not fair for us to act while other countries contributing to the problem are sitting still.”

   

Despite political polarization reaching a historic high in recent years, Americans aren’t divided on ensuring clean air and water for every community. As the U.S. celebrates Earth Day, many Americans say the holiday holds personal importance and want the U.S. to prioritize clean air and water for all and take ambitious action on climate change.

Survey Methodology

From April 17 to 20, 2026, Data for Progress conducted a survey of 1,085 U.S. likely voters nationally using web panel respondents. The sample was weighted to be representative of likely voters by age, gender, education, race, geography, and recalled presidential vote. The survey was conducted in English. The margin of error associated with the sample size is ±3 percentage points. Results for subgroups of the sample are subject to increased margins of error. Partisanship reflected in tabulations is based on self-identified party affiliation, not partisan registration. For more information please visit dataforprogress.org/our-methodology.

media:content.media:title: This Earth Day, Two-Thirds of Voters Say Ensuring Clean Air and Water for All Should Be a Top Priority

item.title: Voters in Multiple States Say Iran War Benefits Israel and That U.S. Military Aid to the Country Should Be Halted
item.dc:creator: Abby Springs
item.description: Across all four states, Independent voters consistently side against weapons transfers to Israel and continued funding for Israel’s military actions, which they believe include a genocide in Gaza.
item.content:encoded:
On April 15, U.S. senators took votes to block the transfer of weapons and bulldozers to Israel. 

In collaboration with the Institute for Middle East Understanding, Data for Progress conducted surveys of likely voters in four states — California, Colorado, Arizona, and Michigan — and finds that most voters in each of these states support blocking transfers of weapons and bulldozers to Israel. Furthermore, majorities of voters in each of the states polled believe Israel benefits more from war with Iran than the United States does.

Voters were asked whether they approve or disapprove of the Senate stopping the transfer of bombs used in Israel’s military operations in Gaza, Iran, and Lebanon. The survey finds that a majority of voters across each state approve of a Senate vote to block this transfer.

   

This support also includes the majority of Democrats and Independents in each state:

  • 67% of California voters, including 79% of Democrats, 76% of Independents, and 35% of Republicans 

  • 61% of Colorado voters, including 85% of Democrats, 60% of Independents, and 36% of Republicans 

  • 58% of Arizona voters, including 74% of Democrats, 62% of Independents, and 35% of Republicans 

  • 53% of Michigan voters, including 67% of Democrats, 60% of Independents, and 30% of Republicans

   

Then, voters were asked whether they approve or disapprove of the Senate stopping the transfer of bulldozers to Israel. The survey finds that a majority of voters across each state approve of a Senate vote to block this transfer:

  • 65% of California voters, including 77% of Democrats and 72% of Independents 

  • 57% of Colorado voters, including 78% of Democrats and 58% of Independents 

  • 54% of Arizona voters, including 73% of Democrats and 55% of Independents

  • 52% of Michigan voters, including 65% of Democrats and 59% of Independents

   

More than half of voters in each state say the war against Iran benefits Israel more than it does the United States:

  • 66% of California voters say Israel benefits more, including 74% of Democrats and 70% of Independents

  • 58% of Colorado voters, including 78% of Democrats and 60% of Independents

  • 54% of Arizona voters, including 72% of Democrats and 53% of Independents

  • 54% of Michigan voters, including 71% of Democrats and 61% of Independents

   

Voters across the four states, including a majority of Independents in each state, agree that Israel is committing a genocide against the Palestinian people in Gaza:

  • 58% of California voters agree that Israel is committing a genocide in Gaza, including 74% of Democrats and 68% of Independents

  • 53% of Colorado voters, including 84% of Democrats and 52% of Independents

  • 50% of Arizona voters, including 76% of Democrats and 53% of Independents

  • 46% of Michigan voters, including 66% of Democrats and 53% of Independents

   

The survey additionally finds that funding the war in Iran is deeply unpopular. Majorities of voters in each state surveyed, including majorities of Independents, strongly oppose Congress funding the U.S. military’s operations in Iran, as well as sending funds to Israel for its military actions in Iran and Lebanon. 

The findings together show that most voters in key states, including Independent voters, are opposed to U.S. involvement in escalating violence in the Middle East.

Across all four states, Independent voters consistently side against weapons transfers to Israel and continued funding for Israel’s military actions, which they believe include a genocide in Gaza. 

Survey Methodology

Data for Progress conducted surveys of likely voters in four states using SMS and web panel respondents: California (n=521, April 8–13, 2026), Arizona (n=562, April 6–12, 2026), Michigan (n=540, April 7–12, 2026), and Colorado (n=541, April 6–13, 2026). Each sample was weighted to be representative of likely voters by age, gender, education, race, geography, and recalled presidential vote. The surveys were conducted in English. The margin of error associated with each sample is ±4 percentage points.

media:content.media:title: Voters in Multiple States Say Iran War Benefits Israel and That U.S. Military Aid to the Country Should Be Halted

item.title: Voters Believe Legal Immigration Generally Helps the U.S.
item.dc:creator: Abby Springs
item.description: Among those who are concerned about illegal immigration, the majority want a safer, more orderly process, not simply fewer immigrants.
item.content:encoded:
In office, President Donald Trump has led a historic mass deportation campaign and pushed for sweeping restrictions on both illegal and legal immigration. But new polling from Data for Progress finds that likely voters hold nuanced views on the reasons for and effects of illegal immigration — and think legal immigration generally helps the U.S.

When asked to identify up to two primary motivations for people crossing the U.S. border illegally, voters most commonly point to economic hardship and danger. Half of voters (50%) say people are fleeing poverty and seeking economic opportunity, and 38% say people are fleeing violence, persecution, or war. About one-third of voters (35%) believe people cross illegally because they want to take advantage of U.S. government services. 

A majority of Republicans (56%) cite government services as a primary motivation, compared with just 14% of Democrats and 31% of Independents. Democrats, meanwhile, are far more likely to cite humanitarian reasons: 53% say people are fleeing violence, compared with only 22% of Republicans.

   

There is strong, bipartisan support for legal immigration. Around two-thirds of voters (66%) say legal immigration generally helps the United States, compared with just 9% who say it generally hurts. This view holds across party lines: 71% of Democrats, 70% of Independents, and 59% of Republicans say legal immigration helps the country.

A majority of voters (62%) say illegal immigration generally hurts the United States, while only 11% say it helps and 22% say it neither helps nor hurts. 

   

Among the 62% of voters who say illegal immigration generally hurts the country, most are primarily concerned about the process by which people are immigrating, rather than the number of people crossing the border.

A majority of these voters (57%) say their main concern is that they want a safer, more orderly process, while 41% say their main concern is that they want lower numbers of immigrants overall. 

This split holds across partisan lines: 62% of Democrats, 60% of Independents, and 54% of Republicans who see illegal immigration as harmful prioritize the process over the numbers. 

   

When asked what effect increasing legal immigration would have on illegal immigration, half of voters (50%) say it would make no difference, and an additional 31% say it would cause illegal immigration to decrease. Just 13% think expanding legal immigration would cause illegal immigration to increase.

   

While the Trump administration has focused solely on reducing the number of immigrants in the United States, by both curbing illegal immigration and making legal pathways harder to access, these findings indicate that voters have more nuanced views. Among those who are concerned about illegal immigration, the majority want a safer, more orderly process, not simply fewer immigrants. And most voters, including most Republicans, do not believe that expanding legal immigration would make illegal crossings worse.

Survey Methodology

From March 27 to 30, 2026, Data for Progress conducted a survey of 1,228 U.S. likely voters nationally using web panel respondents. The sample was weighted to be representative of likely voters by age, gender, education, race, geography, and recalled presidential vote. The survey was conducted in English. The margin of error associated with the sample size is ±3 percentage points. Results for subgroups of the sample are subject to increased margins of error. Partisanship reflected in tabulations is based on self-identified party affiliation, not partisan registration. For more information, please visit dataforprogress.org/our-methodology.

media:content.media:title: Voters Believe Legal Immigration Generally Helps the U.S.

item.title: Voters Disapprove of Trump on Iran as Independents Back His Removal Over "Dying Civilization" Threat
item.dc:creator: Lew Blank
item.description: In mid-March, 49% of Independent voters disapproved of Trump's handling of the conflict with Iran. Today, that disapproval has risen to 70%.
item.content:encoded:
As negotiations with Iran over an end to the war continue, President Donald Trump's handling of the war is drawing growing public disapproval. New polling from Data for Progress finds that Trump's approval on Iran has declined since March — and a clear majority of Independents say Trump should be removed from office over his threat to destroy Iranian civilization. 

In mid-March, voters disapproved of Trump's handling of the conflict with Iran by a -9-point net margin. Today, that opposition has grown: 54% disapprove, while 41% approve (−13 net).

Disapproval among Independents has grown by 21 points, from 49% disapproval in March to 70% disapproval in mid-April.

   

On April 7, President Trump made the following statement about Iran: "A whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again." 

When asked whether Trump should be removed from office for the statement, voters favor removal 50% to 45%, including 84% of Democrats and 61% of Independents.

   

In a question posed to Democrats and Democratic-leaning Independents, voters were asked to evaluate the Democratic Party’s opposition to the war in Iran. A strong majority (61%) of these voters say the Democratic Party is not doing enough to stand up to Trump's war in Iran, and needs to fight harder. More than 1 in 3 (35%) say the party could do better, but is overall doing a decent job.

   

These findings reveal an electorate that is increasingly opposed to Trump's management of the Iran conflict. Independents have moved sharply against Trump on Iran since March, with majorities backing his removal over his April 7 threat. Additionally, the data suggests that there is an opening for Democrats among their base to do more to oppose the war.

Survey Methodology

From April 9 to 14, 2026, Data for Progress conducted a survey of 1,301 U.S. likely voters nationally using web panel respondents. The sample was weighted to be representative of likely voters by age, gender, education, race, geography, and recalled presidential vote. The survey was conducted in English. The margin of error associated with the sample size is ±3 percentage points. Results for subgroups of the sample are subject to increased margins of error. Partisanship reflected in tabulations is based on self-identified party affiliation, not partisan registration. For more information please visit dataforprogress.org/our-methodology.

media:content.media:title: Voters Disapprove of Trump on Iran as Independents Back His Removal Over "Dying Civilization" Threat

item.title: Voters Support a Green Economic Populism Policy Agenda
item.dc:creator: Abby Springs
item.description: Voters agree that climate and the economy are issues that can and should be addressed together.
item.content:encoded:
As climate change strains already-stretched household budgets with billion-dollar extreme weather events, effective climate action must deliver household-level economic security. Yet, despite what some narratives suggest, climate policy doesn’t have to drive up costs to achieve better environmental outcomes. 

The Climate and Community Institute’s new agenda articulates how to achieve such climate policy through the lens of Green Economic Populism, an approach that aims to improve the lives of the working class via kitchen-table climate interventions, like home insurance rate caps and free transit, and broader economic policies, like corporate regulation and green jobs programs.

New polling conducted in February by Data for Progress and the Climate and Community Institute shows voters agree that climate and the economy are issues that can and should be addressed together: 7 in 10 voters believe it is possible for economic policy to lower costs while directly reducing emissions, compared with only 19% of voters who don’t think it’s possible. 

   

Baseline climate and economic views

Voters are drawing a more direct connection between extreme weather and rising costs than they are between climate change and rising costs. In a split sample test, which randomized whether respondents saw the phrase “issues like natural disasters, heat waves, and prolonged droughts” or “climate change,” 80% of respondents say extreme weather events somewhat or greatly impact the rising cost of living, compared with only 61% who say the same about climate change. 

   

Voters are experiencing the rising cost of living across the board, with 86% of respondents facing higher grocery costs and 82% facing higher electricity and gas costs. At least 3 in 5 voters also report higher health care (68%) and home insurance costs (60%). 

Voters’ experiences with rising prices also map onto their expectations about future rising costs. In the next year, at least 7 in 10 voters expect to pay more for groceries (79%), home electricity and gas (77%), and health care (70%), while 63% of voters expect to see rising home insurance costs. Notably, these expectations come from a survey that fielded before the current conflict in Iran, which is raising gas prices and is expected to cause further sticker shock for consumers in the coming months. 

   

There is no clear consensus on what is responsible for the rising cost of living associated with climate change. Around 1 in 5 voters place blame on “U.S. government spending and regulations” (21%) and “climate-related disruptions that are happening right now” (18%). An additional 20% say they do not associate climate change with the rising cost of living at all, while 14% blame “corporate pricing decisions.” 

Policy preferences around the cost of living and climate 

While there may not be consensus among voters around the main drivers of rising prices associated with climate change, 7 in 10 voters agree that climate interventions can reduce the cost of living. Furthermore, a majority of voters (55%) would prefer that elected officials address both the cost of living and climate change through coordinated policies, while 35% want to see elected officials focus primarily on the cost of living and 4% say the same about climate change.

   

A similar majority of voters (56%) think that the government should “expand its involvement” in the issue of climate change and the cost of living via new policies, investments, and regulations. Only 26% think the government should “reduce its involvement” by defunding and deregulating climate policies and relying more on the private sector to lower costs.

   

More specifically, voters consider it important for elected officials to take action on key economic and climate priorities, including nearly universal agreement about the importance of lowering the cost of living (96%) and protecting people and their homes from natural disasters (91%). Voters also view ensuring the affordability of home insurance (89%), the reduction of harmful pollution (88%), the strength of the public sector (84%), and environmental sustainability (82%) as critically important priorities.

   

When provided with a list of potential statements that a candidate might use when campaigning on climate and economic issues, 72% of voters find the following statement a “very persuasive” or “somewhat persuasive” reason to vote for a candidate running for office: “By helping the public sector invest in climate and economic policies, we will make corporations and the wealthy pay their fair share, speed up the shift to clean energy, create good-paying jobs, and lower energy costs for working families.” While other statements tested are considered similarly persuasive overall, including statements focused solely on climate action and solely on economic policies, this statement pairing both climate and economic policy ambitions has the strongest intensity of support among all four tested statements, with 35% considering it “very persuasive.” 

These findings demonstrate that voters are feeling the rising costs of living associated with climate change and want their government to get more involved. Notably, a majority of voters believe climate interventions can reduce the cost of living, and would prefer elected officials to address both issues at once, rather than defunding and deregulating climate policies and relying on the private sector to lower costs. Furthermore, an overwhelming majority of voters say they are facing higher electricity and gas costs, and they believe extreme weather has impacted the rising cost of living.

Grace Adcox (@GraceAdcox) is the senior climate strategist at Data for Progress.

Survey Methodology

From February 20 to 24, 2026, Data for Progress conducted a survey of 1,122 U.S. likely voters nationally using web panel respondents. The sample was weighted to be representative of likely voters by age, gender, education, race, geography, and recalled presidential vote. The survey was conducted in English. The margin of error associated with the sample size is ±3 percentage points. Results for subgroups of the sample are subject to increased margins of error. Partisanship reflected in tabulations is based on self-identified party affiliation, not partisan registration. For more information please visit dataforprogress.org/our-methodology.

media:content.media:title: Voters Support a Green Economic Populism Policy Agenda

item.title: MAHA Lacks a True Base and Voters Reject Its Real-World Impact
item.dc:creator: Abby Springs
item.description: While many voters say they support its general goals, very few identify with the MAHA movement itself, and around 1 in 3 outright reject it.
item.content:encoded:
The Make America Healthy Again (MAHA) movement, led by Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., has gained attention as a key political force following President Donald Trump’s victory in 2024. However, a new national survey from Data for Progress and RFK Jr Watch, a project of 314 Action, finds its support is narrow and highly vulnerable.

While many voters say they support MAHA’s general goals, less than 1 in 6 actually identify with the movement itself, and support drops significantly when voters learn about RFK Jr.’s actions in office and his policies.

Across key groups, including Independents and swing district voters (who were both oversampled), attitudes toward RFK Jr. and aligned Republican candidates decline sharply after exposure to his record, particularly on issues like rising health care costs, cuts to medical research, and changes to vaccine policy. These findings suggest that MAHA’s appeal is not durable, and weakens quickly when voters hear about its real-world consequences.

The survey asked a national sample of 2,350 likely voters which public health figures they do and do not trust, how they feel about the MAHA movement, and whether they support or oppose various public health proposals. The survey finds:

  • RFK Jr. starts the survey underwater, with a plurality of voters saying they are unfavorable of him (-8). By the end of the survey, after voters learn about his actions as health secretary, his favorability drops among every tested subgroup of voters, most by double-digit margins. “Soft MAHA” voters — those who are supportive of MAHA goals but do not identify with the movement  — decrease the most (-18 points), while Independents also decrease significantly (-17 points).

  • When voters are asked directly whether they would support a Republican candidate aligned with RFK Jr.’s public health approach, initial support is already net negative, and declines sharply after voters learn more about his record — from net -12 to net -36 overall, including a drop from -22 to -51 among Independents.

  • The most effective critiques of RFK Jr’s actions as health secretary focus on his failure to stand up to Big Pharma and lower costs, funding cuts for Medicaid and medical research, firing public health experts, and upending vaccine guidance. 

  • Voters prefer candidates with backgrounds in science and medicine over those aligned with the MAHA agenda — by more than a 15-point margin, with far stronger overall support and much less opposition. 314 Action was founded 10 years ago to elect STEM candidates to office. 

  • Voters strongly trust doctors, scientists, and vaccines, while MAHA government officials, podcasters, and health influencers on social media have the lowest levels of trust. 

  • MAHA lacks a true base: Less than 1 in 6 voters identify with the movement, while roughly one-third outright reject it.

  • About half of Independents (51%) and swing district voters (50%) say MAHA is not delivering on its promises.

Trust of Key Figures in Public Health

First, the survey presented voters with various health-related figures and organizations, and asked them about their level of trust in each.

Voters nearly unanimously trust nurses (92%), their personal or family doctor (90%), and doctors in general (86%). More than 2 in 3 voters also trust scientists (79%) and medical researchers at major universities (76%).

Meanwhile, a minority of voters say they trust RFK Jr. (40%), Dr. Oz (36%), Mike Tyson (29%), and “podcasters or health influencers on social media” (29%).

   

Perceptions of the MAHA Movement

Nearly 2 in 5 voters say they support many of MAHA’s goals but do not identify with the movement (39%), including half of Republicans (50%) and 40% of swing district voters. Only 14% of voters consider themselves to be part of the MAHA movement, including 29% of Republicans and just 13% of swing district voters.

More than 1 in 3 voters (34%) — including 33% of swing district voters — do not support the MAHA movement. 

   

The survey also provided respondents with various candidate traits, and asked them whether they would be more or less likely to vote for a candidate for elected office with each trait. Candidates with a background in science, technology, engineering, or math (+58) or who are doctors or nurses (+55) perform better than candidates who support the Make America Healthy Again agenda (+11) or come from a family of politicians (-28).

Broadly, voters respond most favorably to candidates for public office who support removing harmful toxins from food (more likely to support this candidate by a +76-point margin), protecting federal funding for medical research (+69), and paid family and medical leave (+66). Candidates who support limiting the use of pesticides in agriculture (+61), making sure kids are not overmedicated (+61), and requiring all students in public schools to receive vaccines for certain diseases (+57) also perform well.

   

RFK Jr. Opposes Most Popular Public Health Policies, Including Research and Childhood Vaccinations

RFK Jr. has publicly opposed three of the most popular policies that voters support in this survey, including funding public research for cures (88% support, 64% strongly support), requiring routine childhood vaccines (79% support, 54% strongly support), and limiting pesticide use in agriculture (76% support, 47% strongly support). 

The least popular policies include a variety of funding cuts, promotion of herd immunity, and limiting access to Medicare or Medicaid. These trends generally hold among swing district voters and “soft MAHA” voters — those who do not identify as MAHA but say they support the movement’s goals — as well. 

   

About Half of Independents and Swing Voters Say MAHA Failed to Deliver on Promises

When provided with two contrasting statements about the MAHA movement, about half of Independents (51%) and swing district voters (50%) say that MAHA is not delivering on its promises. Overall, more voters (49%) side with a statement about the MAHA movement not delivering on its promises than with a statement about the movement being common sense and bringing necessary change (43%). 

   

How Information About RFK Jr.’s Past Actions Affects Voter Favorability

In the survey, voters were informed about nine actions taken by RFK Jr., including that he “withdrew longstanding guidelines for routine childhood vaccines,” “fired thousands of public health experts,” “claimed autism is linked to vaccines and Tylenol,” and “used artificial intelligence (AI) to cite fake health studies.”

Before receiving this information, voters were about evenly split on their favorability toward RFK Jr., with a majority of Republicans viewing him favorably, and a majority of Democrats and a plurality of Independents rating him unfavorably. By the end of the survey, more than 3 in 5 voters view RFK Jr. unfavorably, including 66% of Independents and 86% of Democrats. 

By the end of the survey, every tested subgroup decreases its favorability of RFK Jr., most by double-digit margins. “Soft MAHA” voters — those who are supportive of MAHA goals but do not identify with the movement — decrease the most (-18 points). Independents (-17), Latino voters (-12), Black voters (-12), white voters (-11), Republicans (-11), and Democrats (-10) also decrease by double-digit margins. 

   

Evaluating the Most Effective Criticisms of RFK Jr.’s Tenure

Data for Progress and RFK Jr. Watch also conducted a message test to identify the messages that voters find most convincing as criticisms of RFK Jr.’s handling of public health.

The top-performing message, which ranks as the most convincing message among Democrats, Independents, Republicans, and “soft MAHA” voters, focuses on cost and corporate greed:

  • Cost: Americans are paying more and more for health care and prescription drugs while pharmaceutical corporations rake in record profits. Instead of standing up to Big Pharma, RFK Jr.’s policies are making it harder to lower costs and protect families’ pocketbooks.

Messages that focus on Medicaid cuts and funding cuts to public medical research round out the top three most effective public health messages:

  • Medicaid: Republican lawmakers, with the support of RFK Jr. and the Trump administration, passed the largest cuts to Medicaid in history, putting rural hospitals and community clinics at risk of closure. Families in small towns and working-class communities could lose access to the care they depend on.

  • Research Cuts: Under RFK Jr., billions of dollars in funding for cancer, Alzheimer’s, and other medical research have been cut, halting clinical trials and delaying potential cures. Patients and families are paying the price for political decisions.

The best-performing message among swing district voters specifically relates to how the U.S. once led the world in public health innovation:

  • Global Leader: The U.S. used to be a respected leader in the scientific community, making and sharing research discoveries that changed the world. Now, we are no longer a part of the World Health Organization and we have canceled research studies. We are losing our reputation as innovative leaders.

Weaker messages connect RFK Jr. with President Donald Trump, describe his hypocrisy around pesticide use, and discuss the profits he has made from trademarking MAHA.

Please see the Survey Methodology appendix below to read the full language for all 11 messages.

   

Conclusion

This survey demonstrates that support for the MAHA movement is shallow. While many voters say they support its general goals, very few identify with the movement itself, and around 1 in 3 outright reject it. Support for MAHA declines significantly as voters learn more about RFK Jr.’s actions as health secretary, particularly on issues like health care costs, cuts to medical research, and vaccine policy. Messages that highlight these real-world impacts, especially around affordability and standing up to Big Pharma, are among the most effective.

Among swing district voters, this dynamic is especially clear. While many express initial openness to MAHA’s goals, they do not believe the movement is delivering on its promises and strongly prefer candidates with backgrounds in science or medicine over those aligned with the MAHA agenda. At the same time, voters place overwhelming trust in doctors, nurses, scientists, and vaccines, while expressing low trust in MAHA-aligned figures and influencers.

These findings highlight clear vulnerabilities for RFK Jr. and the MAHA movement. Voters strongly oppose cuts to Medicaid and medical research, and continue to support policies like funding research for cures and requiring routine childhood vaccinations.

For candidates, including those in competitive and Republican-leaning districts, this creates a clear opportunity: Voters are receptive to contrast messaging that focuses on the real-world consequences of RFK Jr.’s policies — particularly rising costs, reduced access to care, and threats to scientific research.

Cover photo attributable to Gage Skidmore.

Survey Methodology

From March 5 to 12, 2026, Data for Progress and RFK Jr. Watch, a project of 314 Action, conducted a survey of 2,350 likely voters nationally using web panel respondents. The sample was weighted to be representative of likely voters by age, gender, education, race, geography, and recalled presidential vote. The survey was conducted in English. The margin of error associated with the sample size is ±2 percentage points. Results for subgroups of the sample are subject to increased margins of error. Partisanship reflected in tabulations is based on self-identified party affiliation, not partisan registration. For more information please visit dataforprogress.org/our-methodology.

This sample is also weighted to account for oversamples of self-identified Independents and swing districts voters with unweighted Ns of 1,121 and 499, respectively, to ensure proportional representation of likely voters. 

“Swing districts” here are defined as congressional districts identified by the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee as “Districts in Play” or “Frontline.”

media:content.media:title: MAHA Lacks a True Base and Voters Reject Its Real-World Impact

item.title: Voters — Including Most Republicans — Oppose Cutting Health Care to Fund War
item.dc:creator: Abby Springs
item.description: A majority of Democrats (84%), Independents (69%), and Republicans (55%) oppose cutting health care to fund the Iran War.
item.content:encoded:
As a presidential candidate, Donald Trump promised that he was “not going to start a war” and that money for wars in the Middle East would be better spent at home. Now, as president, Trump has launched a war in Iran and is demanding an additional $200 billion for the war, funded directly through cuts to health care.

In a new survey, Data for Progress finds that a strong majority of voters (69%) oppose congressional Republicans’ proposal to reduce health care spending in order to provide $200 billion in funding for the Iran War, while only 22% support this proposal.

A majority of Democrats (84%), Independents (69%), and Republicans (55%) oppose cutting health care to fund the Iran War.

   

Public Opinion on Cuts to Medicaid

In order to cut health care spending, Republicans in Congress are specifically looking to target Medicaid. House Majority Leader Steve Scalise stated on March 30 that Republicans’ health care cuts will be targeted “especially in the areas of fraud and waste and abuse” — nearly identical to the language Republicans used last year to justify nearly $1 trillion in Medicaid cuts under the One Big Beautiful Bill. In reality, only about 3% of the bill’s cuts to Medicaid actually addressed fraud, according to the Congressional Budget Office.

The survey asked voters which is more important to them: protecting care for people on Medicaid or combatting Medicaid fraud. A majority (51%) say they prioritize “making sure everyone who needs Medicaid can access care, even if that means some taxpayer money is lost to fraud.”

Meanwhile, 39% prioritize “making sure no taxpayer money is wasted on fraud in Medicaid, even if that means some people who need care temporarily lose access.”

   

In February, the Trump administration withheld $259 million in Medicaid funding to the state of Minnesota due to allegations of fraud.

A majority of voters (59%) think that if the federal government finds high rates of improper payments in a state’s Medicaid program, it should keep funding the program while requiring the state to fix the problem. Only about 1 in 3 voters (32%) would prefer the federal government withhold funding from the state until it fixes the problem.

   

Additionally, the survey finds that voters strongly oppose multiple proposals to cut Medicaid, including capping the total amount of funding each state receives (-33-point net margin), eliminating payments to family members who serve as caregivers for disabled relatives (-46), and eliminating home care services for seniors and people with disabilities (-58).

   

Together, these findings indicate that voters across party lines oppose cutting health care spending to fund the war in Iran, more specifically oppose various potential cuts to Medicaid, and prioritize protecting Medicaid spending over combatting fraud.

Survey Methodology

From April 3 to 6, 2026, Data for Progress conducted a survey of 1,213 U.S. likely voters nationally using web panel respondents. The sample was weighted to be representative of likely voters by age, gender, education, race, geography, and recalled presidential vote. The survey was conducted in English. The margin of error associated with the sample size is ±3 percentage points. Results for subgroups of the sample are subject to increased margins of error. Partisanship reflected in tabulations is based on self-identified party affiliation, not partisan registration. For more information, please visit dataforprogress.org/our-methodology.

media:content.media:title: Voters — Including Most Republicans — Oppose Cutting Health Care to Fund War

item.title: Voters Want $200 Billion Spent on Domestic Priorities Like Health Care, Not War
item.dc:creator: Abby Springs
item.description: Two in three voters (66%) say $200 billion in spending should be used for domestic programs. Only 28% say it should fund the war in Iran.
item.content:encoded:
The Pentagon has estimated that the first week of the war with Iran cost the U.S. more than $11.3 billion. And after a month, Congress is debating President Donald Trump’s request for $200 billion in additional spending to fund the war. 

New polling from Data for Progress finds that most voters do not see the war in Iran as a good use of taxpayer dollars, and that they would rather see funds directed toward domestic priorities like health care.

After learning that the Trump administration recently requested $200 billion in additional funding for the Department of Defense to fund the war in Iran, nearly 3 in 5 voters (58%) say the spending would be a bad use of U.S. taxpayer money.

   

When asked directly whether the $200 billion in requested funding should go toward the war in Iran or toward domestic social programs like health care, 2 in 3 voters (66%) say it should be used for domestic programs. Only 28% say it should fund the war.

Republicans are split on this question: 47% say the money should fund the war, and 47% say it should go to domestic programs.

   

Additionally, a majority of voters (56%) think that the Trump administration has not done enough to explain why the U.S. is at war with Iran. Only 27% of Independents believe the administration has done enough to make a case for the war. 

   

Broadly, these findings indicate that the Trump administration's request for $200 billion in supplemental funding faces significant public opposition. A majority of voters do not think the administration has done enough to explain why the U.S. is at war with Iran, and they’d rather see it direct funding toward domestic social programs like health care. 

Survey Methodology

From March 27 to 30, 2026, Data for Progress conducted a survey of 1,228 U.S. likely voters nationally using web panel respondents. The sample was weighted to be representative of likely voters by age, gender, education, race, geography, and recalled presidential vote. The survey was conducted in English. The margin of error associated with the sample size is ±3 percentage points. Results for subgroups of the sample are subject to increased margins of error. Partisanship reflected in tabulations is based on self-identified party affiliation, not partisan registration. For more information, please visit dataforprogress.org/our-methodology

media:content.media:title: Voters Want $200 Billion Spent on Domestic Priorities Like Health Care, Not War

item.title: New York Voters Want Big Oil to Pay for the Home Insurance Crisis
item.dc:creator: Abby Springs
item.description: New York voters are already burdened by the home insurance crisis — and believe the fossil fuel industry helped create it.
item.content:encoded:
As the extreme weather impacts of climate change intensify, insurers across the country are hiking premiums, dropping policyholders, and leaving markets entirely. In New York, the question of who should bear those costs has become increasingly urgent, as state lawmakers consider proposals to hold the fossil fuel industry financially accountable for the extreme weather disasters that have driven insurers to the brink.

Data for Progress and the Center for Climate Integrity recently surveyed 648 likely voters across New York state to understand how voters are experiencing rising insurance and housing costs, what they believe is driving those costs, and how they view proposals to make large oil and gas corporations pay.

Housing costs are rising for New Yorkers on multiple fronts. More than half of New York voters (54%) say their home or renters insurance bill has risen over the past year, including 68% of homeowners. 

At the same time, 4 in 10 voters (41%) say their mortgage or rent payment has increased, with more than half of renters (54%) saying their rent has gone up.

   

Those rising costs are already leading some New Yorkers to reconsider their current home insurance coverage. More than 1 in 4 voters (27%) say that rising insurance costs have led them to research other providers or policies, and 1 in 10 have gone so far as to switch providers. 

A majority of New York voters (56%) also say state lawmakers should focus more on ensuring homeowner's insurance is affordable, compared with just 28% who say the state should continue its current approach and 4% who say it should focus less. That call for action is bipartisan: 55% of Democrats, 51% of Independents, and 61% of Republicans all say the state should do more on insurance affordability.

   

As home insurance rates rise, New York voters also perceive the number of extreme weather events to be rising as well. Nearly two-thirds of New York voters (64%) say the number of extreme weather events — like flash floods, hurricanes, heat waves, and droughts — has increased over the past five years. Democrats are most likely to say such events have increased (79%), but a majority of Independents (57%) and a plurality of Republicans (47%) agree.

Broadly, New Yorkers hold the fossil fuel industry responsible for climate change and worsening extreme weather. Nearly 7 in 10 New York voters (69%) say large oil and gas companies are very or somewhat responsible for climate change and worsening extreme weather disasters.  This includes 85% of Democrats, 64% of Independents, and even a slim majority of Republicans (52%).

   

Additionally, two-thirds of New York voters (67%) support legislation that would allow the state attorney general to sue the oil and gas industry to recover home insurance cost increases — rather than letting insurers pass those costs onto New Yorkers. Just 24% oppose the proposal, a +43-point net margin statewide. 

Support is strong across partisan lines: 86% of Democrats back the proposal, as do 58% of Independents and a plurality of Republicans (48%).

   

The proposal's support holds up even after voters are exposed to arguments on both sides. When respondents first are presented with an argument in favor — that oil and gas companies have known for decades their products worsen extreme weather, and misled the public about it — support rises to 71% (+50-point net margin). 

When respondents are then presented with an argument in opposition — that companies will simply pass costs through to consumers via higher energy prices — a majority of voters (58%) still support the bill.

   

Broadly, these findings indicate that New York voters are already burdened by the home insurance crisis — and believe the fossil fuel industry helped create it. With bipartisan majorities calling on their lawmakers to do more, and strong bipartisan support for making oil and gas companies pay for climate-driven insurance cost increases, the data suggests New York lawmakers have clear public backing to pursue accountability legislation.

Survey Methodology

From March 5 to 13, 2026, Data for Progress conducted a survey of 648 likely voters in New York using web panel respondents. The sample was weighted to be representative of likely voters by age, gender, education, race, geography, and recalled presidential vote. The survey was conducted in English. The margin of error associated with the sample size is ±4 percentage points. Results for subgroups of the sample are subject to increased margins of error. Partisanship reflected in tabulations is based on self-identified party affiliation, not partisan registration. For more information, please visit dataforprogress.org/our-methodology.

media:content.media:title: New York Voters Want Big Oil to Pay for the Home Insurance Crisis

item.title: Montgomery County Democratic Primary Voters Back Housing Growth and Walkable Communities
item.dc:creator: Abby Springs
item.description: Nearly two-thirds (62%) of Montgomery County voters say the county should encourage new construction or modernization to ensure residents have access to up-to-date housing.
item.content:encoded:
The cost of housing in Montgomery County, Maryland, has skyrocketed over the last several years, as the rate of homelessness in the county has risen while permitting for new multifamily development has steeply declined. However, new polling from Data for Progress and Greater Greater Washington reveals paths toward a solution: A majority of Montgomery County Democratic primary voters view the drop in multifamily permitting as a serious problem and want to see new housing construction in the county.

By a +6-point margin, Montgomery County Democratic primary voters say they would be more likely to support a council member who backs a proposed housing development in their neighborhood. A plurality of Democratic primary voters (33%) say they would be more likely to vote for that council member in their reelection, compared with 27% who say they would be less likely, and another 29% who say it would not affect their vote at all.

Democratic primary voters under 45 back the pro-housing council member by a +50-point net margin, while voters 45 say they would be less likely to support such a council member by a net of −11. Renters are strongly on board (+34 net), while homeowners are mildly opposed (−6 net). 

   

When told that the number of permits for new multifamily buildings in Montgomery County fell from over 2,000 in 2024 to about 50 in 2025, a majority of Democratic primary voters (54%) call the decline a major issue. That concern is expressed similarly across demographics, including age and homeownership.

   

Given a direct choice between protecting neighborhood character and enabling new housing, Democratic primary voters lean clearly toward growth. Nearly two-thirds (62%) say the county should encourage new construction or modernization to ensure residents have access to up-to-date housing, even if neighborhoods may look or feel different. Just 29% say the county should limit new construction to protect the existing look and feel of neighborhoods.

   

However, when it comes to adding housing on their own block, Democratic primary voters are more split: 47% say they are either very or somewhat comfortable with new development, while 35% say they are not comfortable at all. Comfort climbs to 57% in their neighborhood or subdivision, 68% in their ZIP code, and 78% somewhere else in Montgomery County. 

Young Democratic primary voters and renters are more comfortable with housing built on their own block: 72% of voters under 45 are comfortable with this kind of housing, as are70% of renters.

   

When it comes to walkability and road safety, 74% of Democratic primary voters say the county should redesign streets to improve safety for all users, even if it slows drivers down and increases traffic. Only 16% say the county should prioritize making it easier and quicker for drivers to get where they're going, even if it is less safe.

   

When asked what matters more to them personally — living within walking distance of daily needs or having more space at home — Montgomery County voters prioritize walkability. Sixty-three percent say it is more important to live near shops, parks, and public transit, even if it means less space, while 31% say more space matters more, even if that means living farther from those amenities.

   

Taken together, these results indicate that Montgomery County Democratic primary voters are not opposed to housing growth — they see the current construction slowdown as a serious problem and would prefer to see politicians prioritize building new multifamily housing with walkability in mind. 

Survey Methodology

From February 10 to 19, 2026, Data for Progress conducted a survey of 409 likely Democratic primary voters in Montgomery County, Maryland, using SMS and web panel respondents. The sample was weighted to be representative of likely voters by age, gender, education, race, and geography. The survey was conducted in English. The margin of error associated with the sample size is ±5 percentage points. Results for subgroups of the sample are subject to increased margins of error. Partisanship reflected in tabulations is based on self-identified party affiliation, not partisan registration. For more information, please visit dataforprogress.org/our- methodology.

media:content.media:title: Montgomery County Democratic Primary Voters Back Housing Growth and Walkable Communities

item.title: Voters Disapprove of U.S. Strikes in Iran, and a Plurality Think Israel Has Too Much Influence Over U.S. Foreign Policy
item.dc:creator: Abby Springs
item.description: A narrow plurality of voters overall (43%) feel that the Israeli government has “too much” influence over the U.S. foreign policy agenda.
item.content:encoded:
The U.S.-Israel war on Iran, which began on February 28, has killed more than 1,200 Iranians and seven U.S. service members, among others. The Trump administration has offered conflicting accounts of the role Israel played in the U.S.’ decision to strike Iran.

In a new survey, Data for Progress, in partnership with the Institute for Middle East Understanding and Demand Progress, asked U.S. likely voters whether Israel has the right amount of influence on U.S. foreign policy, whether a war with Iran benefits the U.S. or Israel more, and whether Trump’s strikes on Iran have made them more or less likely to vote for a Republican in the upcoming midterm elections. The survey also tested the effectiveness of a hypothetical Democrat who defends the United States’ military relationship with Israel, compared with one who prioritizes domestic concerns.

Data for Progress first asked voters about the Israeli government’s role in determining U.S. foreign policy priorities. The survey finds that a narrow plurality of voters overall (43%) feel that the Israeli government has “too much” influence over the U.S. foreign policy agenda, while 41% think it has “the right amount” of influence and only 5% think it has “too little” influence. This is driven by a majority of Democrats, 59% of whom believe the Israeli government has too much influence over U.S. foreign policy, alongside 48% of Independents. In contrast, only 22% and 36% of Democrats and Independents, respectively, feel that the Israeli government has the right amount of influence over U.S. foreign policy.

   

Next, voters were informed that “Secretary of State Marco Rubio has said the U.S. had to launch the war against Iran now because Israel was going to attack Iran anyway, which would cause Iran to respond by attacking U.S. forces in the region.” They were then asked whether launching a war in Iran benefits the U.S. or Israel more. A majority of voters (56%) — including majorities of Democrats (67%) and Independents (56%), and a plurality of Republicans (47%) — say that the war in Iran benefits Israel more than the U.S.

   

Voters were then asked how the recent military strikes against Iran might affect their vote choice in the upcoming midterm elections. The survey finds that a plurality of voters overall say that they are less likely to vote for a Republican candidate in the midterm elections as a result of Trump ordering the strikes. Much of this is once again driven by Democrats and Independents, the former of whom, unsurprisingly, overwhelmingly report being less likely to vote Republican (72%), alongside a narrow majority of Independents (51%).

   

Next, Data for Progress ran a split test to see how different foreign policy stances might affect a hypothetical matchup between candidates of either party. In the first split, both the Democrat and the Republican place a strong emphasis on standing with Israel, including by means of providing military support for its operations in Gaza and Iran. When both candidates take similar stances on this issue, a plurality of voters say they would support the Republican over the Democrat, with another 20% either being undecided or saying they would sit out this election. In particular, Independents who choose a candidate show a slight preference for the Republican candidate, while nearly a quarter, along with 10% of Democrats, say that they would not vote in an election between two such candidates, allowing for a narrow Republican victory.

   

In the second split, the Republican once again prioritizes providing military support for Israel, while the Democrat now emphasizes funding domestic policy priorities — “even if that means reducing support for the military actions of Israel.” In this hypothetical, the survey finds that the Democrat beats their Republican opponent by more than 10 points, with significant shifts away from non-voting and toward the Democratic candidate among both Democrats and Independent voters. The survey even finds a small uptick in support for the Democrat among Republican voters when the candidate takes this stance. 

   

Finally, Data for Progress asked likely Republican primary voters specifically how this same difference in foreign policy stances would affect their vote choice in a hypothetical primary election. The survey finds that Republican primary voters are relatively split between a candidate who prioritizes militarily funding Israel (48%) and one who prioritizes funding domestic policy concerns (45%). Notably, among Republican-leaning Independents, the matchup is even tighter, with these voters being twice as likely to report being undecided and to sit out this election entirely, compared with likely Republican primary voters overall. Taken together, even in a Republican primary, taking an unwavering stance on militarily supporting Israel will not guarantee an easy win.

   

Overall, these findings indicate that more than 2 in 5 voters think Israel has too much influence over U.S. foreign policy and that voters would be less likely to support a Republican candidate in the midterm elections as a result of President Trump’s strikes. After hearing a statement from Secretary Rubio about U.S. motivations for striking Iran, a majority of voters also say that the war benefits Israel more than the U.S. Our split test finds that a hypothetical Democratic candidate who supports the U.S.’ military relationship with Israel would lose a race to a hypothetical Republican candidate, while a Democrat who prefers to focus on issues at home — even if this means reducing military support for Israel — would defeat this candidate.

Survey Methodology

From March 6 to 8, 2026, Data for Progress, in partnership with the Institute for Middle East Understanding and Demand Progress, conducted a survey of 1,251 U.S. likely voters nationally using web panel respondents. The sample was weighted to be representative of likely voters by age, gender, education, race, geography, and recalled presidential vote. The survey was conducted in English. The margin of error associated with the sample size is ±3 percentage points. Results for subgroups of the sample are subject to increased margins of error. Partisanship reflected in tabulations is based on self-identified party affiliation, not partisan registration. For more information please visit dataforprogress.org/our-methodology.

media:content.media:title: Voters Disapprove of U.S. Strikes in Iran, and a Plurality Think Israel Has Too Much Influence Over U.S. Foreign Policy

item.title: Voters Strongly Oppose Boots on the Ground in Iran, Think the War Is Not Worth It
item.dc:creator: Lew Blank
item.description: In our most recent survey, 69% of voters say they are paying more for gas — a 23-point increase from last year.
item.content:encoded:
The war with Iran has entered its third week. While President Donald Trump has at times stated that the war will end “very soon,” on other occasions he has suggested it will last longer and has privately expressed interest in placing U.S. boots on the ground.

In a new survey, Data for Progress finds that a strong majority of voters (68%) would oppose the U.S. deploying soldiers on the ground in Iran, while only about 1 in 4 voters (26%) would support it. Democrats (85%) and Independents (71%) are strongly unified in opposition, while Republicans are evenly split, with 48% in support and 48% opposed.

   

When asked what they consider to be the main reason for the U.S. carrying out military strikes against Iran, voters are widely split. About 1 in 4 respondents attribute the U.S. strikes mainly to defending America’s national security (28%) or to pursuing American economic and energy interests (25%), while more than 1 in 10 attribute the strikes to advancing Israeli interests (16%) or promoting human rights and democracy in Iran (14%).

There is notable variation in these results by partisanship.
